item.title: Attribute failure to the earliest broken step
item.dc:creator: Yee Seng Chan
item.description:
Part of a series

Reliability from first principles

  1. Measure before you optimise
  2. Attribute failure to the earliest broken step

A single accuracy number is a thermometer: it tells you the patient is sick, not why. Once you have traces (Part 1), the useful operation is to bucket each failed run by its earliest broken step.

Earliest, not most visible

The visible symptom is almost always the last step — a confidently wrong answer. But if retrieval returned nothing useful, the answer was doomed three steps earlier. Attribute the failure to retrieve, not to the model’s final call. Optimising the visible step is how teams spend a quarter making the wrong thing better.

What tends to fall out of this, once you actually do it, is that most failures are deterministic and yours to fix — which is the good news the flat number was hiding.

item.title: Measure before you optimise
item.dc:creator: Yee Seng Chan
item.description:
Part of a series

Reliability from first principles

  1. Measure before you optimise
  2. Attribute failure to the earliest broken step

You cannot improve what you cannot attribute. The first move in any reliability effort is not a better model or a cleverer prompt — it is making the system’s behaviour legible enough that a failure can be pinned to a cause.

The minimal trace

For every run, capture four things per step: what went in, what came out, whether the step met its contract, and a free-text note when it didn’t. That’s enough to ask the only question that matters at this stage — where did this run actually die?

An agent you can’t trace isn’t unreliable. It’s unfalsifiable, which is worse, because you can still ship it.

In the next part we turn those traces into an attribution that tells you what to build next.
